Title: Tomato SlCER1-1 catalyzes the synthesis of wax alkanes, increasing drought tolerance and fruit storability

Plant_Genes.Notes_or_Other_Important_Info: SlCER1-1 did not exhibit a clear substrate chain-length preference and contributes to synthesis of C25–C35 n-alkanes and branched alkanes; VLC alkanes were reduced but not completely absent in SlCER1-1 RNAi organs, indicating functional redundancy with other SlCER1 homologs. The slcer1-1 mutant showed weaker effects on floral organ fusion and high-chain-length alkanes than the slcer6 mutant, consistent with a less severe disruption of VLC alkane synthesis.
Plant_Genes.Summary_key_Findings_of_Core_Gene:
1. SlCER1-1 encodes a FAH superfamily/WAX2 C-terminal domain protein localized to the ER and is highly expressed in wax-producing tomato tissues (leaves, fruits, stems), with strong induction by drought and ABA.
2. Overexpression of SlCER1-1 in Arabidopsis markedly increases total cuticular wax load, specifically elevating C25–C35 n-alkanes and branched alkanes, confirming its catalytic role in VLC alkane formation.
3. RNAi knockdown of SlCER1-1 in tomato (slcer1-1) drastically reduces VLC alkanes in leaves, fruits, stems, and flowers, increases aldehydes and primary alcohols, and greatly decreases total wax loads.
4. Under drought and postharvest storage, SlCER1-1-deficient plants show impaired drought tolerance, higher cuticular water loss, lower relative water content, and severely crinkled fruits, demonstrating that SlCER1-1-mediated VLC alkane biosynthesis is critical for cuticular barrier function, drought tolerance, and fruit storability.
Plant_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: SlCER1-1 is a key positive regulator of VLC alkane accumulation in tomato cuticular wax; its activity increases n-alkane and branched alkane levels in leaves, fruits, stems, and flowers, thereby reducing cuticular permeability, enhancing drought tolerance, and improving fruit storability. Loss of SlCER1-1 function greatly reduces VLC alkanes, increases cuticular water loss, and sensitizes plants and fruits to drought and desiccation.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Mechanism: ER-localized aldehyde decarbonylase activity converting very-long-chain aldehydes to VLC alkanes without clear chain-length preference between C25 and C35 and using both straight-chain and branched substrates; reduced SlCER1-1 activity diverts substrates toward aldehydes and primary alcohols.
Plant_Genes.Regulatory_Pathway: Functions in the alkane-forming branch of cuticular wax biosynthesis and is transcriptionally induced during drought and ABA treatment as part of the drought stress response pathway.
Plant_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Cuticular wax very-long-chain (C25–C35) n-alkane and branched alkane biosynthesis via the alkane-forming pathway, catalyzing conversion of aldehydes to VLC alkanes in tomato cuticles.
Plant_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: FAH superfamily domain (PF04116) and WAX2 C-terminal domain (PF12076) containing three His-rich motifs
Plant_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Endoplasmic reticulum (ER), based on SlCER1-1–eGFP fusion colocalizing with ER marker mCherry-HDEL in Nicotiana benthamiana leaf epidermal cells
Plant_Genes.Expression_Pattern: Highly expressed in leaves, fruits, and stems but not roots; transcript levels in leaves higher at 80 days than at 30 days; higher expression in orange-stage fruits than immature green fruits; strongly induced in leaves by drought (34-fold at 12 h) and ABA (4-fold at 12 h), moderately affected by cold and dark treatments.
Plant_Genes.Key_Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Drought stress imposed by withholding water; ABA treatment (100 μM) applied by spraying; cold treatment at 4°C; continuous dark treatment; postharvest fruit storage at room temperature to assess water loss.
Plant_Genes.Research_Topic: Cuticular wax biosynthesis and composition, drought stress response, and fruit storability in tomato.
Plant_Genes.Trait_Category: Abiotic stress tolerance (drought tolerance), cuticular water permeability, and fruit storability/postharvest water loss.
Plant_Genes.Quantitative_Phenotypic_Alterations: Overexpression in Arabidopsis increased total leaf wax load by 20.1%, 48.75%, and 65.84% in lines 1, 2, and 22, respectively; n-alkanes increased by 79.03%–184.34% and branched alkanes by 21.91%–186.48%, with no obvious changes in other wax classes. In SlCER1-1 RNAi tomato lines, total cuticular wax content decreased by ~70% in leaves, 50% in fruits, 53% in stems, and 45% in flowers, with predominant reductions in C27–C33 n-alkanes and C29–C33 branched alkanes; leaf and fruit fatty acids also decreased. Aldehydes in leaves increased by 87.29% (line 5) and 109.23% (line 19), and primary alcohols in stems increased by 121.61% (line 5) and 162.47% (line 19). Under drought, wild-type leaves showed a strong increase in total wax load and in C27–C33 n-alkanes and C29, C31, C33 branched alkanes, whereas RNAi lines showed no significant change. RNAi plants exhibited higher leaf water loss and faster chlorophyll leaching and significantly lower relative water content after 13 days of water deprivation, and fruits from RNAi plants showed markedly higher water loss over 21 days of storage.
Plant_Genes.Other_Phenotypic_Effects: SlCER1-1 knockdown increased aldehyde levels in leaf wax and primary alcohols in stem wax, consistent with reduced conversion of aldehydes to alkanes and a shift toward alcohol-forming pathways; RNAi plants showed increased cuticular permeability evidenced by faster chlorophyll leaching, crinkled leaf edges under drought, and severely crinkled fruits after storage.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Materials: Tomato (Solanum lycopersicum) cv. Micro Tom wild-type plants; SlCER1-1 RNAi tomato lines 2, 5, and 19 (reduced VLC alkane mutant slcer1-1); Arabidopsis thaliana Col-0 plants overexpressing SlCER1-1 (lines 1, 2, 22); Nicotiana benthamiana leaf epidermal cells for transient expression.
Plant_Genes.Experimental_Methods: Homology search and Pfam domain analysis; phylogenetic and gene structure analysis; qRT-PCR for tissue-specific and stress-induced expression profiling; promoter cis-element prediction; generation of SlCER1-1 RNAi tomato lines and SlCER1-1-overexpressing Arabidopsis lines via Agrobacterium-mediated transformation; subcellular localization using 35S:SlCER1-1/eGFP in Nicotiana benthamiana; GC-MS and GC-FID analysis of cuticular wax composition and chain-length distribution; drought, ABA, cold, and dark treatments; leaf water loss and chlorophyll leaching assays; relative water content measurement; fruit storage water loss assays under room temperature.
Plant_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Functional validation by generating SlCER1-1 RNAi knockdown tomato lines (slcer1-1) and analyzing wax composition and drought-related traits; heterologous overexpression of SlCER1-1 in Arabidopsis leaves followed by wax profiling; subcellular localization of SlCER1-1–eGFP fusion; drought and postharvest storage phenotyping of RNAi lines versus wild type.
Plant_Genes.Gene_Discovery_or_Cloning_Method: Identified as a CER1 homolog by BLASTP search of the tomato genome using Arabidopsis CER1, with confirmation of FAH superfamily and WAX2 C-terminal domains by Pfam; CDS amplified from 80-day-old tomato leaf cDNA and cloned into 35S-driven vectors for overexpression, subcellular localization, and RNAi constructs.
Plant_Genes.Breeding_Application_Value: Because SlCER1-1 promotes VLC alkane accumulation, reduces cuticular water loss, and enhances drought tolerance and fruit storability, increasing SlCER1-1 activity or selecting alleles that boost VLC alkanes via conventional breeding or gene editing is a promising strategy to improve drought resilience and extend shelf life of tomato fruits. The slcer1-1 mutant provides a useful model for dissecting CER1 homolog function and designing breeding targets.
